summary:
Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi (c. 780–850 AD), a scholar at the [[event:2136|House of Wisdom]] in Baghdad, is the father of algebra — a mathematician whose two most important works gave European mathematics both its name and its most powerful computational tool. His Arabic treatise 'Kitab al-mukhtasar fi hisab al-jabr wal-muqabala' (c. 830 AD) — 'The Compendious Book on Calculation by Completion and Balancing' — systematized the solving of linear and quadratic equations using methods that could be applied mechanically without needing geometric proofs; the word 'algebra' derives from 'al-jabr' in his title. His second major work introduced the Hindu-Arabic decimal numeral system (including zero) to the Islamic world in a form that was subsequently transmitted to Europe.

The word 'algorithm' derives from the Latin transliteration of al-Khwarizmi's name (Algoritmi), reflecting his role as the systematizer of step-by-step computational procedures. His mathematical works were translated into Latin in the 12th century and became standard texts in European universities — meaning that every European mathematician for the next 500 years learned their basic arithmetic and algebra from al-Khwarizmi's methods. He also produced geographical works (improving on Ptolemy's coordinates for 2,402 locations) and astronomical tables that were similarly influential. Al-Khwarizmi's contributions were the essential bridge between Indian mathematical innovation (the decimal system, zero) and European mathematical development — without him, the Scientific Revolution would likely have occurred much later if at all.
